query: How can a tax-exempt entity verify if its refueling property location is within an eligible census tract using 2020 census boundaries?
ground_truth_answer: To verify if a tax-exempt entity's refueling property location is within an eligible census tract using 2020 census boundaries, the entity must first determine its 11-digit census tract geographic identifier (GEOID) using the Census Bureau mapping tool. After obtaining the GEOID, the entity should check if it is included in the IRS-provided list in Appendix B.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 6029 Tax-Exempt Entities

query: Are payments for prescription medicines and drugs deductible as medical expenses?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, payments for prescribed medicines and drugs can be included as medical expenses. A prescribed drug is one that requires a doctor's prescription for an individual's use. Additionally, amounts paid for insulin can also be included, even if not prescribed. However, nonprescription drugs, with the exception of insulin, cannot be included in medical expenses.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 502 Medical and Dental Expenses

query: What method is used to determine the fair market value of a donated easement when evidence of comparable easement sales is unavailable?
ground_truth_answer: When evidence of comparable easement sales is unavailable, the "before-and-after" valuation method is used to determine the fair market value of a donated easement. This method calculates the fair market value by subtracting the value of the restricted land with the easement (the "after value") from the value of the unfettered land before the restriction (the "before value").
ground_truth_doc_id: 10–20 U.S. Tax Court Opinions

query: What are the standard deduction amounts for the 2025 tax year?
ground_truth_answer: For the 2025 tax year, the standard deduction amounts are: $15,750 for single filers or those married filing separately; $31,500 for those married filing jointly or qualifying as a surviving spouse; and $23,625 for heads of household. These amounts are for most people and do not apply if you were born before January 2, 1961, are blind, or can be claimed as a dependent by someone else.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 17 – Your Federal Income Tax

query: What are the requirements and rules for claiming the Child Tax Credit (CTC) for the 2025 tax year?
ground_truth_answer: For the 2025 tax year, the Child Tax Credit (CTC) can reduce your tax by up to $2,200 for each qualifying child. A child is considered qualifying if they meet seven conditions: 1. Relationship (son, daughter, stepchild, foster child, brother, sister, or descendant of any of these). 2. Age (under 17 at the end of 2025). 3. Support (did not provide over half of their own support). 4. Residency (lived with you for more than half of 2025, with exceptions for temporary absences, birth/death in 2025, kidnapped children, children placed for adoption, eligible foster children, and children of divorced/separated parents). 5. Dependency (claimed as a dependent on your return). 6. Filing Status (does not file a joint return, unless only to claim a refund). 7. Citizenship (U.S. citizen, U.S. national, or U.S. resident alien). An adopted child is always treated as your own. To claim the CTC, the child must have a Social Security Number (SSN) valid for employment, issued on or before the due date of your 2025 return (including extensions). If the child's SSN was not valid for employment but became eligible by the due date, the CTC can still be claimed. The Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) is available if you cannot claim the full CTC. Refunds for returns claiming the ACTC cannot be issued before mid-February 2026. The CTC and ACTC may be reduced if your modified adjusted gross income (AGI) exceeds $400,000 for those married filing jointly, or $200,000 for all other filing statuses. If you or your spouse (if filing jointly) do not have an SSN or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) issued by the due date of your 2025 return (including extensions), you cannot claim the CTC or ACTC.
ground_truth_doc_id: 20 IRS Publications

query: How much combat zone pay can commissioned officers exclude per month for the year 2025?
ground_truth_answer: For the year 2025, commissioned officers (excluding commissioned warrant officers) can exclude up to $10,983 per month of their combat zone pay. This amount is composed of $10,758 for the highest enlisted pay and $225 for imminent danger pay.

query: What is a Trump account, how is it established, and what are the eligibility requirements for the $1,000 pilot program contribution?
ground_truth_answer: The "Trump account" is a new type of traditional individual retirement account (IRA) established for the exclusive benefit of a child. To establish this account and potentially receive a $1,000 pilot program contribution from the U.S. Treasury, Form 4547 must be filed. A child is eligible for the $1,000 pilot program contribution if they were born after December 31, 2024, and before January 1, 2029, are a U.S. citizen, have not had a prior pilot program contribution election processed, and possess a valid Social Security Number (SSN).
ground_truth_doc_id: Instructions for Form 4547Form

query: What are the deduction limits and eligibility requirements for the "No tax on tips" and "No tax on overtime" deductions?
ground_truth_answer:
For the "No tax on tips" deduction, you cannot deduct more than $25,000 of qualified tips. Your deduction will be limited if your modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) is more than $150,000 (or $300,000 if married filing jointly). To be eligible, you and/or your spouse who received the tips must have a valid SSN, and if married, you must file a joint return.

For the "No tax on overtime" deduction, you may be eligible to deduct up to $12,500 ($25,000 if married filing jointly) of your qualified overtime compensation. Your deduction will be limited if your MAGI is more than $150,000 ($300,000 if married filing jointly). To be eligible, you and/or your spouse who received the overtime must have a valid SSN, and if married, you must file a joint return.
ground_truth_doc_id: Publication 3 (2025), Armed Forces' Tax Guide

query: How much qualified passenger vehicle loan interest paid or accrued in 2025 can be deducted, and what are the phase-out limits?
ground_truth_answer: For qualified passenger vehicle loan interest paid or accrued in 2025, you may be able to deduct up to $10,000 of that interest. query: What is the overall limit for the deduction of state and local income, sales, and property taxes for the 2025 tax year?
ground_truth_answer: For 2025, the overall limit for the deduction of state and local income, sales, and property taxes has been increased to $40,000 (or $20,000 if married filing separately). This limit is reduced if your modified adjusted gross income exceeds $500,000 (or $250,000 if married filing separately), but it will not be reduced below $10,000 (or $5,000 if married filing separately).

query: What changes have been made to the adoption credit refundability and special needs determinations beginning in 2025?
ground_truth_answer: Beginning in 2025, up to $5,000 of the adoption credit may be refundable, with the refundable portion determined separately for each eligible child. Additionally, Indian Tribal governments now have parity for special needs adoption determinations, meaning their determinations are recognized for adoption credit purposes, similar to state government determinations.

query: What is the standard mileage rate for the business use of a car, van, pickup, or panel truck for 2025, and what are the rules regarding deducting actual expenses?
ground_truth_answer: For 2025, the standard mileage rate for the business use of a car, van, pickup, or panel truck is 70 cents per mile. This rate covers the cost of operating the vehicle for business purposes. If you choose to use the standard mileage rate, you cannot deduct your actual expenses for that year, with the exception of business-related parking fees and tolls. However, you may still be able to deduct interest paid on a car loan.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 17

query: What are the depreciation limitations for passenger automobiles placed in service in calendar year 2025?
ground_truth_answer:
For passenger automobiles placed in service in calendar year 2025, the depreciation limitations are as follows:
- If the Code section 168(k) additional first-year depreciation deduction applies: $20,200 for the first tax year, $19,600 for the second tax year, $11,800 for the third tax year, and $7,060 for each succeeding year.
- If the Code section 168(k) additional first-year depreciation deduction does not apply: $12,200 for the first tax year, $19,600 for the second tax year, $11,800 for the third tax year, and $7,060 for each succeeding year.
These limits are reduced if the business use of the vehicle is less than 100%.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 463 – Travel, Gift, and Car Expenses pdf download - Copy

query: How does tax law distinguish between entertainment expenses and business meals when food is provided at an entertainment venue?
ground_truth_answer: Tax law distinguishes between entertainment expenses and business meals when food is provided at an entertainment venue based on how the food and beverages are purchased and documented. Generally, entertainment expenses are not deductible. However, 50% of the cost of business meals may be deductible if certain conditions are met, such as the taxpayer or an employee being present, the food not being lavish or extravagant, and being provided to a current or potential business contact. Food and beverages provided during entertainment events are not considered entertainment if they are purchased separately from the entertainment, or if their cost is stated separately from the entertainment cost on one or more bills, invoices, or receipts. If these conditions are met, 50% of the cost of the food and beverages may be deductible as a business meal expense. It is important not to inflate the amount charged for food and beverages to circumvent the entertainment disallowance rule.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 463 – Travel, Gift, and Car Expenses pdf download - Copy

query: What distinct legal criteria must a business expense meet to be classified as both ordinary and necessary for a tax deduction?
ground_truth_answer: To be classified as both "ordinary" and "necessary" for a tax deduction, a business expense must meet distinct legal criteria. An ordinary expense is defined as one that is common and accepted in your field of business. A necessary expense is one that is helpful and appropriate for your business; it does not have to be indispensable to be considered necessary.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 334 – Tax Guide for Small Business

query: Under what conditions is an employee exempt from using Publication 463 to report business-related travel and transportation expenses?
ground_truth_answer: An employee is exempt from using Publication 463 to report business-related travel and transportation expenses if all of the following conditions are met: the employee fully accounted to their employer for work-related expenses, received full reimbursement for those expenses, was required by their employer to return any excess reimbursement and did so, and there is no amount shown with a code L in box 12 of their Form W-2, Wage and Tax Statement.

query: For a business that does not have an applicable financial statement, what is the maximum per-invoice dollar threshold allowed to deduct the cost of tangible property under the de minimis safe harbor rules?
ground_truth_answer: For a business that does not have an applicable financial statement, the maximum per-invoice dollar threshold allowed to deduct the cost of tangible property under the de minimis safe harbor rules is $2,500 per item or invoice.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 334 – Tax Guide for Small Business

query: What are the structural and length-of-service requirements for an employee achievement award to be considered deductible tangible personal property?
ground_truth_answer: To be considered deductible tangible personal property as an employee achievement award, the award must meet specific structural and length-of-service requirements. Structurally, the award must be tangible personal property and cannot be cash, a gift certificate, or an equivalent item. The employer must present the award as part of a meaningful presentation, under conditions and circumstances that do not create a significant likelihood of it being disguised pay. For length-of-service awards, the employee must have at least 5 years of service, and must not have received another length-of-service award during the current year or the previous four years.
ground_truth_doc_id: RS Publication 525 – Taxable and Nontaxable Income

query: How is business rent paid multiple years in advance treated for a cash method taxpayer, and is there an exception?
ground_truth_answer: For a cash method taxpayer, business rent paid multiple years in advance generally must be capitalized and deducted only in the year to which it applies, rather than in the year it was paid. Specifically, if an advance payment creates an intangible asset, such as prepaid rent, it must be capitalized and amortized over the appropriate period. However, an exception exists: if the right or benefit created does not extend beyond the earlier of 12 months after the date the right or benefit is first received, or the end of the tax year following the year in which the advance payment was made, then a cash method taxpayer can generally deduct the entire amount when paid.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 535 – Business Expenses - Copy

query: Can a business deduct employee student loan repayments as educational assistance, and when does this provision expire?
ground_truth_answer: Under the expanded CARES Act educational assistance provisions, a business can generally deduct the cost of employee student loan repayments as fringe benefits. This exclusion applies to employer payments of principal or interest on any qualified education loan incurred by the employee for their education, whether paid directly to the employee or to a lender. This specific tax exclusion is set to expire on January 1, 2026, unless it is extended by future legislation.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 535 – Business Expenses - Copy

query: What are the 2025 annual contribution limits for an employee participating in a 401(k) plan, including catch-up contributions for older employees, and are qualified wildfire relief payments taxable?
ground_truth_answer: For 2025, the annual contribution limits for an employee participating in a 401(k) plan are $23,500. If the employee is age 50 or older, the limit increases to $31,000. For those age 60 through 63 at the end of 2025, the limit is $37,750. Qualified wildfire relief payments are not considered taxable income.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 17 – Your Federal Income Tax

query: Can a married taxpayer filing a separate return claim the Credit for the Elderly or the Disabled if they lived with their spouse during the tax year?
ground_truth_answer: No, a taxpayer who is married and files a separate return cannot claim the Credit for the Elderly or the Disabled if they lived with their spouse at any time during the tax year.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 17 – Your Federal Income Tax

query: What are the rules and penalty exceptions for a broker voluntarily reporting basis information for a digital asset classified as a noncovered security on Form 1099-DA?
ground_truth_answer: A broker is permitted to voluntarily report basis information for a digital asset classified as a noncovered security without being subject to penalties under sections 6721 or 6722 if they check box 9 on Form 1099-DA to indicate that the digital assets are noncovered securities. If box 9 is checked, the broker is not required to complete boxes 1d, 1g, 1h, 1i, and 6, and does not need to check box 2. However, if the broker chooses to complete boxes 1d, 1g, 1h, 1i, 2, and 6 for a noncovered security voluntarily, they will not face penalties for incorrect reporting or furnishing of this information.
ground_truth_doc_id: Instructions for Form 1099-DA

query: What is the fee and typical processing time for requesting a copy of a tax return using Form 4506, and is the fee refundable if the return cannot be found?
ground_truth_answer: The processing fee for each return requested using Form 4506 is $30. The IRS typically takes up to 75 calendar days to process this request. If the requested tax return cannot be located, the fee will be refunded.
ground_truth_doc_id: Form 4506 Request for Copy of Tax Return

query: What formatting and required statement rules apply if a single signature line is used for both tax certifications and unrelated business provisions on a substitute Form W-9?
ground_truth_answer: If a single signature line is used for both the required tax certifications and other unrelated business provisions on a substitute Form W-9, the certifications must be highlighted, boxed, printed in bold-face type, or otherwise made to stand out. Additionally, the following statement must appear immediately above the single signature line, also made to stand out: “The IRS does not require your consent to any provision of this document other than the certifications required to avoid backup withholding.”
ground_truth_doc_id: Instructions for the Requester of Form W-9

query: What is the backup withholding rate for reportable payments made after 2017 when a payee fails to provide a Taxpayer Identification Number or provides an obviously incorrect TIN?
ground_truth_answer: For reportable payments made after 2017, the backup withholding rate is 24% when a payee fails to provide a Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) or provides an obviously incorrect TIN.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 7951 Backup witholding

query: What form should a payer use to report backup withholding, and where can they find information on deposit requirements?
ground_truth_answer: A payer should use Form 945, Annual Return of Withheld Federal Income Tax, to report backup withholding. For information on deposit requirements for Form 945, consult the instructions for Form 945 or Publication 15 (Circular E), Employer’s Tax Guide.
ground_truth_doc_id: IRS Publication 7951 Backup witholding

query: What is the penalty assessed under section 6702(a) for a “frivolous tax return”?
ground_truth_answer: The penalty assessed under section 6702(a) for a “frivolous tax return” is $5,000.
ground_truth_doc_id: Microsoft Word - RR-07-19

query: Is compensation received in exchange for personal services (wages) considered taxable income under section 61(a)(1) of the Internal Revenue Code?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, compensation received in exchange for personal services, commonly known as wages, is considered income under section 61(a)(1) of the Internal Revenue Code and is subject to federal income tax. Arguments that such compensation is not taxable income have been consistently rejected by courts and are considered frivolous. The Sixteenth Amendment to the Constitution grants Congress the power to levy taxes on income from any source, including wages, without apportionment among the states.
ground_truth_doc_id: Microsoft Word - RR-07-19

query: How did total municipal bond issuances change from calendar year 2022 to calendar year 2023?
ground_truth_answer: Total municipal bond issuances decreased by 1.3% from $456.4 billion in Calendar Year 2022 to $450.3 billion in Calendar Year 2023.
